core_intuitions: A single generation conflates intrinsic ability with sampling luck; Pass@k reframes coding ability as a probability, and the unbiased estimator is a sampling-without-replacement counting argument: 1 - C(n-c,k)/C(n,k) is the chance that a random draw of k from the n generated samples contains ZERO of the c passers.
The naive estimator 1-(1-c/n)^k overestimates Pass@k for small n, and Pass@k can DECREASE as k grows toward n when a problem has c=0 (no passers to ever draw).

Ground truth for code is behavior, not text: correctness must be verified by RUNNING the code, and running untrusted model code demands an isolation broker (Docker/gVisor/Firecracker) with resource caps.

Math answers need symbolic equivalence, not string match ('1/2' == '0.5'); and formal-proof benchmarks sidestep equivalence heuristics entirely because a proof assistant's kernel either type-checks the proof or it does not.

A right final answer can come from a flawed chain and a perfect chain can end in an arithmetic slip; ORMs score only the outcome while PRMs score every step, so evaluating the trace catches failure modes the answer alone hides.

Test-time compute is now a dial, so a single accuracy number is uncomparable: the honest artifact is an accuracy-vs-token-budget CURVE, and when two models' curves cross the better model is budget-dependent.

Both training and eval share the same verifiers, so every gameable shortcut (contamination, format exploitation, unit-test hardcoding, agent self-report) corrupts the signal; defenses are oracle-based checks and held-out/temporal test sets.

existing.name: rcae-sandbox-architecture
existing.issue: Reads cleanly in both themes: the vertical flow Eval Orchestrator -> Sandbox Manager (isolation broker) -> Isolated Container (resource caps) -> verdict pills (pass/fail/TLE/OOM) conveys exactly the 'run untrusted code behind an isolation broker with caps' intuition. Edge labels (submit code + tests / spawn isolated process / stdout, exit code) make the data flow explicit. Dark-theme keeps a couple of light inner cards that do not invert, but text stays legible; not worth disturbing.

existing.name: rcae-frontier-measurement-stack
existing.issue: The linear stack Raw Benchmark -> Prompt Template -> Sampling Config -> Execution/Verification -> Aggregation -> Comparison & Reporting with the vertical brace 'every stage shifts the reported number' nails the chapter's thesis that a benchmark number is the product of a pipeline of choices, not an intrinsic property. Legible in both themes. Optional-only nit: in dark mode the inner box body fill is quite dark with dark-gray body text (borderline contrast on the sub-caption lines); bumping body text to the theme foreground token would tighten it, but it is readable as-is.

new_figures.name: rcae-passk-urn-without-replacement
new_figures.concept: Pass@k as sampling-without-replacement: draw k samples from the n generated, and Pass@k is 1 minus the probability the whole draw lands in the failing pile.
new_figures.rationale: This is the mathematical heart of the chapter's opening and its single most teachable mechanic, yet no figure serves it. Readers memorize the formula 1 - C(n-c,k)/C(n,k) without feeling WHY it is a probability. An urn/two-pile draw diagram turns the combinatorics into a physical intuition: the estimator counts the draws-of-k that miss every passer. It also makes the two counter-intuitive facts land visually: why the naive formula overestimates, and why Pass@k stays 0 forever when c=0.
new_figures.placement: Why a Single Sample Is Not Enough
new_figures.brief: Center a single 'urn' / pool holding n generated samples for ONE problem, using the chapter's illustrative counts n=20 with c=6 passers and n-c=14 failers. Draw the 20 as small chips in two visually distinct groups: 6 chips marked with a check ('passes all tests', an accent-pass color) and 14 chips marked with an x ('fails', a muted/negative color). Label the pool 'n = 20 samples generated for one problem'. To the right, illustrate one DRAW of k (use k=10): a lasso/scoop pulling 10 chips out. Show TWO contrasting draws stacked: (a) a 'bad draw' where all 10 scooped chips come only from the 14-failer subset -> tag 'zero passers drawn -> this problem scored as FAIL'; (b) a 'typical draw' containing at least one checked chip -> tag 'at least one passer -> PASS'. Beneath, present the estimator as a labeled fraction so each piece maps to the picture: Pass@k = 1 - [ C(n-c, k) / C(n, k) ] with a call-out bracket under C(n-c,k) reading 'ways to draw all k from the FAILING pile (the bad draws)' and under C(n,k) 'all possible draws of k'. Add a small side panel 'Two things this picture explains': line 1 -- 'Naive 1-(1-c/n)^k pretends draws are WITH replacement, overcounting bad-draw escapes -> it overestimates for small n'; line 2 -- 'If c=0 the failing pile IS the whole pool, every draw is a bad draw, so Pass@k = 0 no matter how large k gets.' Keep the k=1 sanity note tiny: 'at k=1, Pass@k = c/n = 6/20 = 0.30.' Theme-safe: pass chips use an accent, fail chips a muted foreground; do not hardcode primaries; all integers are the chapter's own illustrative values, no benchmark percentages. Everything must fit the viewBox with clear left-to-right reading order (pool -> draw -> formula -> two-facts panel).

new_figures.name: rcae-accuracy-vs-compute-crossing-curves
new_figures.concept: Accuracy-vs-token-budget curves for two models that cross, showing that with a test-time-compute dial a single accuracy number is uncomparable and the 'better' model is budget-dependent.
new_figures.rationale: Test-time compute being a dial is the chapter's most modern and most counter-intuitive point, and it is currently pure prose (the 80%/75%/95% apples-to-oranges passage and the Acc(B) definition). A crossing-curves plot is the canonical way this intuition clicks: one glance shows that reporting one number hides which regime you measured, and that 'dominates everywhere' vs 'curves cross' are qualitatively different verdicts. It also visually unifies the chapter's later mechanisms (majority vote, best-of-N, ToT) as ways of spending the x-axis.
new_figures.brief: A single line chart. X-axis: 'test-time compute per problem (tokens, log scale)' with illustrative tick labels 1x, 4x, 16x, 64x rather than absolute token counts. Y-axis: 'accuracy' from 0 to a ceiling (no numeric benchmark values on the curve; use gridlines only). Plot TWO model curves, both monotonically rising and concave (diminishing returns), that CROSS once. Model A ('cheap-but-caps-early'): starts higher at low budget, rises fast, then flattens to a lower plateau. Model B ('scales-with-thinking'): starts lower at low budget, keeps climbing, overtakes A past the crossing point. Mark the crossing with a vertical dashed guide labeled 'crossover budget'. Shade/annotate the two regimes: left of crossing 'small budget: Model A wins', right of crossing 'large budget: Model B wins', with a banner payoff: 'A single accuracy number just names a point on this curve -- report the whole curve.' Add two dropped guide lines at, say, the 4x tick showing A above B, and at 64x showing B above A, each with a tiny 'reported as best?' tag to dramatize how vendors can each pick a favorable budget. Small legend distinguishing 'dominates everywhere = unambiguously better' (a faint third dashed curve entirely below both, optional) from 'curves cross = budget-dependent'. Below the plot, a thin strip labeling the levers that move you rightward: 'majority vote (k samples) | best-of-N + verifier | tree-of-thought / MCTS+PRM' with a caption 'ways to spend the x-axis'. Theme-safe: two distinguishable accent hues for the curves that read in light and dark, axis/gridlines in muted foreground; NO invented benchmark percentages -- shape and ordering carry the meaning, not values.

new_figures.name: rcae-prm-vs-orm-trace-scoring
new_figures.concept: ORM scores only the final answer while a PRM scores every step, so the two disagree exactly on the failure modes that matter: right answer via a flawed step, and a perfect chain with a last-step slip.
new_figures.rationale: The 'evaluate the trace, not just the answer' section names two failure modes in prose (lucky-correct-via-flawed-step and perfect-chain-with-arithmetic-slip) that are precisely what a step-scored diagram makes visceral. Showing a reasoning chain annotated with per-step PRM scores next to a single ORM outcome check makes the distinction, and why PRMs surface red flags ORMs cannot, click in one glance. Priority 2: important intuition but a second-tier mechanic relative to Pass@k and the compute curve.
new_figures.placement: Evaluating the Reasoning Trace, Not Just the Answer
new_figures.brief: Two chain-of-thought traces side by side, each drawn as a vertical stack of step cells (Step 1 -> Step 2 -> Step 3 -> Final answer). Right of each chain, two scoring lanes: a PRM lane (a small score/thumb per STEP) and an ORM lane (a single check/x on the FINAL answer only). LEFT trace 'Right answer, wrong reasoning': steps 1-2 fine (PRM high), step 3 contains a flawed move that happens to cancel (PRM flags it low/red), Final answer is correct -> ORM shows a green check ('outcome: PASS') while the PRM lane shows a red step -> call-out: 'ORM says PASS; PRM catches the flawed step -- a reasoning red flag the outcome hides.' RIGHT trace 'Perfect chain, last-step slip': steps 1-3 all PRM-high (all green), Final answer has an arithmetic error -> ORM shows a red x ('outcome: FAIL') while every step scored well -> call-out: 'ORM says FAIL, but the reasoning was sound to the last line.' Between them a compact legend: 'ORM = one score on the final answer (cheap, blind to how). PRM = one score per step (sees the how).' Bottom caption: 'Answer-only grading misses both cases -- run a final-answer check AND a PRM on sampled traces.' Keep step text schematic (short placeholder tokens like a1, a2, sum, not real math) so nothing needs invented content. Theme-safe: high-score green and flag-red as accents that survive both themes; step cells and connectors in muted foreground; fit within viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading in each chain.

summary: The two existing figures are both keeps: rcae-sandbox-architecture cleanly conveys 'run untrusted code behind an isolation broker with resource caps -> verdict', and rcae-frontier-measurement-stack nails the thesis that a reported number is the product of a pipeline of choices (both legible in light and dark; the measurement-stack has an optional-only dark-mode body-text contrast nit). But the chapter's three most foundational intuitions are unvisualized. Proposed new figures: (1, P1) Pass@k as an urn draw-without-replacement, turning the combinatorial estimator 1 - C(n-c,k)/C(n,k) into a physical two-pile counting picture that also explains the naive-estimator overcount and the c=0 monotonicity quirk; (2, P1) accuracy-vs-token-budget crossing curves, the canonical way the test-time-compute-dial 'single number is uncomparable' intuition clicks and which unifies majority-vote/best-of-N/ToT as ways of spending the x-axis; and (3, P2) a PRM-vs-ORM trace-scoring diagram showing the two failure modes (right answer via flawed step; perfect chain with last-step slip) where step-level and outcome-level grading disagree. All briefs use only the chapter's own illustrative integers or schematic placeholders and invent no benchmark numbers.
